comment: BB2001 measurement records (companion to scripts/measure/*.js, mirroring the goldlogs.json pattern). Each record names the src/config.ts constant it informs, so the audit trail runs source -> record -> the constant. Geometry records are in SOURCE pixels (BB2001 renders 640x480). Two independent sources now exist: the public YouTube capture YMmqNpmA60U (measured in-browser with canvas pixel access) and a local ScummVM capture of the Steam re-release (pixel-exact screenshots + a lossless screen recording). Method notes are in docs/research/backyard-2001-video-notes.md; the capture rig is in docs/research/bb2001-capture-setup.md. Every record carries a `status` that scripts/measure/conformance.test.js enforces -- see the statuses block below.

statuses.conformed: BB's value is measured and trusted, and ours is inside the band. The test asserts it stays there.
statuses.known-drift: BB's value is measured and trusted, and ours is OUTSIDE the band. The test asserts the drift is still the recorded SIZE, so it cannot grow or be half-fixed unnoticed. Closing one means editing the record -- a visible, reviewed act.
statuses.awaiting-measurement: BB's value is NOT yet measured (or rests only on a superseded reading). The test asserts our value is unchanged from the recorded snapshot and makes no claim about BB. This is how the pending work stays enumerable instead of becoming folklore.
statuses.note: A recorded finding about the MEASUREMENT rather than about a constant -- a trap, a technique, a dead end. No constant to conform to, but too expensive to rediscover.

schemaNote: Anything carrying a `category` is a record and must carry a `status`. The `sources` block is provenance, not records, and carries neither.

youtube.note: Public gameplay capture, 640x480. Canvas pixel access confirmed untainted, so geometry was measured objectively (dirt/grass boundary scans + RANSAC foul-line fits), not eyeballed. SUPERSEDED FOR PACE: the notes' timestamps no longer map to the stream (an ad or re-upload shifted the timeline), so no play can be relocated by timestamp. The geometry result is unaffected -- it came from a single self-consistent frame.

local.game: Backyard Baseball '01, Steam app 3104970 (2025 re-release ships the original Humongous data untouched), run under ScummVM on macOS

video.gameRectNote: The recording is of a SCREEN, not of the game: the emulator window fills 1920x1440 of the 3024x1964 frame, i.e. 46.55% of the pixels. Located by detectGameRect (bounding box of the largest inter-frame change at a known hard cut) -- NOT by looking for a non-black region, because the surround is desktop wallpaper. Everything measured off this video is read through crop=1920:1440:552:266, which also means nothing outside the emulator window is ever decoded.

screenshots.note: docs/research/bb2001-capture-setup.md predicted ScummVM would write the raw 640x480 framebuffer. It does not -- it writes the scaled window. The conclusion survives anyway: the blit is an exact 3x nearest-neighbour upscale, so decimating the 3x3 blocks recovers the framebuffer bit-for-bit and COLOUR IS EXACT (no YUV round trip, unlike anything read from the video). Measured across all 12: 99.59-99.96% of blocks perfectly uniform, the remainder being ScummVM's OSD toast and the mouse cursor, both drawn at native resolution over the scaled game.

frameRate.what: The rate at which the PICTURE actually changes, which is the real precision floor -- not the 60fps the container claims.
frameRate.method: distinctFrameRate over high-motion windows (dupFactor 2.7-3.3, medianRunLength 2-3), corroborated by the inter-frame delta cadence during a live play, which steps in a clean +50ms grid.
frameRate.implication: Every timestamp carries +-50ms of quantisation. A ~3000ms run measurement is +-1.7% (fine); a ~250ms pitch flight is +-20% and needs n>=5 to average down. This is WORSE than the YouTube source's ~33ms, and it is the number summarize({framePeriodMs}) must be fed -- passing the container's 16.67ms would claim 3x the precision that exists.
frameRate.note: Recording at 60fps was deliberate oversampling and it paid for itself here: had the capture been made at the assumed rate, the duplication would have been invisible and every derived constant would have inherited a 3x overstatement of precision.

playIndex.what: Where the plays are in the local capture. BB2001 cuts INSTANTLY from the behind-plate view to the wide field view when a ball is put in play, so a scene-cut list is a play index.
playIndex.method: findCuts with crop=gameRect, scale=4, threshold=0.25, run only over the confirmed game segments. Each cut-delimited segment is then labelled plate/wide by median colour in two bottom-of-frame regions (the behind-plate HUD strip paints #004100 there; every wide-field venue paints something else). Calibrated against screenshots 00007/00008 (plate) and 00001/00003/00009 (wide).
playIndex.cutThresholdEvidence: Real cuts score 0.72-0.81 once cropped; ordinary play scores ~0.03. The threshold sits in a wide empty valley rather than being guessed.
playIndex.priorAttempt: An uncropped run at the default 0.3 threshold found 5 cuts in 450 seconds. ffmpeg's scene metric is frame-GLOBAL, so with the game covering 46.55% of the frame every real cut scored ~0.45 -- and only the two strongest cuts in the whole capture cleared 0.3. Cropping first recovered 6.8x more cuts. This is recorded because the failure was silent: a broken index looks exactly like a capture with no plays in it.
playIndex.informs: Nothing in src/ directly. This is the map the pace pass measures against.

gameSegments.what: Which parts of the capture contain the emulator, decided WITHOUT inspecting what any frame depicts.
gameSegments.method: ScummVM blits 640x480 at an integer factor, so inside its window every group of 3 consecutive rows is pixel-identical; native desktop content is not. blitScore samples row-triples (rows, not columns -- yuv422p subsamples chroma horizontally, so column triples break at block boundaries while row triples survive exactly) and scores ONLY samples where the image varies vertically, since a flat region passes the triple test trivially and would let a blank window impersonate the game.
gameSegments.note: 463 samples at 2s spacing, no ambiguous ones and no overlap between the classes. 737s of game material -- 2.3x what an earlier hand-built index had assumed was in scope. Measurement is confined to the two game runs.

projectionType.what: Is BB2001's field an affine squash of the diamond (like ours) or true perspective? A diamond is a SQUARE, so its 4 bases are a square's corners; affine maps preserve diagonal bisection, perspective maps do not.

measurements.method: RANSAC fit of the white chalk foul lines (occlusion-robust), bases from dirt/grass boundary scans so standing fielders read through.

measurements.venue: backyard-grass (pool, shed, red roof) -- the venue every play in the local video is on
measurements.method: Compact white-blob detection at NATIVE resolution (pixel-exact, no scaling), cross-validated across three frames of the same venue: a base cannot move between frames, a fielder can. Two symmetric candidates at y~283 appeared in only one frame and were correctly rejected as fielders; the four that persisted are the bases. Confirmed visually by overlay.
measurements.internalChecks: 1B and 3B land on the SAME y (310.9) without being constrained to; home x 320.3, 2B x 318.0 and the 1B/3B midpoint x 320.45 all agree within 2.5px. Under an affine map opposite legs would be equal -- home-1B is 198.58 and 2B-3B is 176.43, an 11.75% spread, which is an independent perspective signature that does not use the diagonal test at all.

projectionType.nNote: Two INDEPENDENT sources, two different venues, two different measurement techniques, both far past the threshold (11.6x and 5.9x). Perspective STRENGTH differs by venue (0.141 vs 0.082), so it is a per-venue camera property, not one global constant -- worth knowing before anyone tries to model it with a single number.
projectionType.driftNote: Our projection maps y 1:1 -- a pure horizontal pinch with NO vertical foreshortening, so its diagonal-midpoint gap is exactly 0 and it is affine by construction. Matching BB would mean a render-only y'=f(y) plus a matching inverse in unproject, keeping the sim flat so geometry.ts, clampToField's convexity argument and the goldlog stay untouched.
projectionType.deliberate: This drift is a KNOWN PRODUCT DECISION, not an oversight. Our flatter view is a style choice; adding perspective is a large visual change gated behind the graphics phase and does not affect pace.

foulSlope.what: Near-field lateral foreshortening: horizontal run per unit of depth along the foul lines from home.

measurements.method: RANSAC fit of white chalk foul-line pixels, refit on inliers.
Derived from the four cross-validated base positions via lib.js affinity().

foulSlope.nNote: Two sources, two venues, two techniques, agreeing to 0.24%. That agreement is what makes the 3.2% gap to our own value a real difference rather than measurement noise.
foulSlope.driftNote: Ours is 3.2% shallower. Small, and arguably below the threshold anyone would notice -- but it is 14x the spread between the two independent measurements, so it is a genuine difference and not noise. Our near-field LATERAL squash essentially matches BB; what we lack is the DEPTH compression (see geometry.projection-type).

palette.note: Pixel-exact colour is now available (see sources.local.screenshots) and video colour is not -- video carries +-2 per channel from YUV<->RGB conversion even losslessly encoded. Only a first sample is recorded here; a systematic palette pass has not been done.

backyardGrass.what: BB's grass is not one colour -- it is a lit gradient plus a dither, so a region MEDIAN is the meaningful statistic and patchFlatness correctly reports isFlat=false (that is texture, not a filter; the blit check in screenshot.js is what proves the source is unfiltered).
backyardGrass.nNote: Hand-picked sample regions, one frame, one venue. Recorded to establish that the exact-colour path works end to end, NOT as a palette anyone should build against yet.

pace.note: THE PRIORITY, and still open after a measurement attempt (2026-07-23). The shot list (home->1B, fly hang, line drives, fielder/throw delays, pitch corridor) is what fixes the 'too fast' defect. What now exists: a reproducible index of 14 live plays with exact start frames (instrument.play-index), the venue's base positions in the same coordinate space (geometry.projectionType.measurements[1]), a measured 50ms precision floor, and the classification of those plays. What does NOT exist is trustworthy per-play event timing -- session1 is a natural playthrough whose plays do not isolate the shot-list situations, for three concrete reasons recorded in pace.captureConfounds. No pace values are seeded from the superseded n=1 YouTube readings; producing a shaky number here would be the exact mistake this instrument exists to prevent.

measurementTrap.what: Why the obvious automation does not work, recorded so the next pass does not rediscover it.
measurementTrap.finding: Detecting 'the runner reached 1B' from a change-fraction spike in a box on the bag FAILS. BB2001 plays canned fielder animations at the bag, and they are deterministic: six different plays produced change series matching to the millisecond (1.187:0.07, 1.287:0.07, 1.337:0.08, 1.387:0.12, ...). A naive threshold reads six identical 'arrivals'. The tell was the impossible precision -- six unrelated plays cannot agree to 1ms -- and it was caught only because the numbers were inspected rather than aggregated.
measurementTrap.implication: Runner arrival must be distinguished from scripted animation by magnitude AND by the runner's track along the baseline, or read frame-by-frame off a zoomed corridor sheet at the true 50ms period. Either way it is per-play work; it is not a threshold sweep.
measurementTrap.note: This is precisely the failure mode this whole instrument exists to prevent: a plausible, confident, wrong number. It would have produced a home->1B time with a suspiciously tight spread and therefore a HIGH derived confidence.

captureConfounds.what: Why THIS capture (session1) cannot yield clean pace numbers, and what a capture that could must do differently. Found by attempting all three pace metrics on the 14 indexed plays, 2026-07-23.
findings: home->1B lane overlap: in the backyard-grass venue (the only venue any local play is on), the 1B foul line points straight into right-center field. Every ball put in play in this session went to right/center, so the batter-runner's home->1B lane is drawn ON TOP OF the ball-and-fielder action. A frontier tracker along the baseline locks onto the right fielder standing near 1B, not the runner. Clean anchor samples need the ball hit to LEFT field (3B side) or an infield single, so the right-side lane stays empty.

between-pitch is deliberation, not turnaround: the plate-view segments are long (30s+) but the gaps between actual pitches are 5-12s of PLAYER DELIBERATION over the pitch cards (the notes already flag pitch selection as untimed). FLOW.BETWEEN_PITCH_MS models the forced catch->ready dead time (~1.3s), which is a sub-second sliver AFTER each pitch settles -- invisible in the pitch-to-pitch cadence and readable only frame-by-frame per pitch.

few clean flies: the short right porch turns most solid contact into grounders that roll to the fence (play-01 is the type case -- a ball rolling to the wall, retrieved), not arcing flies. FLY_HANG needs deliberate shallow->deep pop-ups, which a natural at-'em playthrough rarely produces.

playClassification.note: From contact-sheet reads. S1 = 0..452s, S2 = 640..925s; all backyard-grass.
quick-out: play-00 (24.1, ~2s -- too short to contain a full home->1B)

captureConfounds.recommendation: A short targeted re-capture following docs/research/bb2001-capture-setup.md step 7 is the clean path, and it is what that step already prescribes ('produce these deliberately instead of hoping they occur'): a handful of home->1B run-outs with the ball poked to LEFT field or an easy infield single, and deliberate pop-ups shallow->deep, each shot name said aloud. Alternatively, per-play frame-by-frame reading of session1's cluttered plays can extract a few low-confidence samples but cannot reach the shot list's n=6.

homeToFirst.what: THE ANCHOR. Every other pace number is a dimensionless ratio against this one, which is what makes the whole approach immune to BB being 640x480 while we are 960x640, and immune to any future tempo dial.

priorReading.why: Single sample, and its timestamp no longer locates the play it came from.

homeToFirst.oursNote: 179.63px at 85px/s = 2113ms in main mode. Against the superseded 3000ms reading we run ~30% fast -- but that comparison is only as good as an n=1 number nobody can relocate, which is exactly why it is not recorded as a drift.

flyHang.what: Deep-fly hang time, as a RATIO to the home->1B anchor. The ratio is the durable form: absolute hang times cannot be compared across two games with different field scales.
flyHang.oursNote: Our worst suspected defect: against the superseded reading our flies hang 42%-106% longer RELATIVE TO THE RUN. Note this was always a ratio claim -- an earlier comment held that liveplay steps on a tempo-scaled delta, but GameScene passes Phaser's raw delta and there is no tempo anywhere in src/, so sim-ms and real-ms are the same thing today. The defect survives that correction unchanged, because both sides of a ratio scale together.

betweenPitch.oursNote: Expected to be the easiest metric; turned out confounded (see pace.captureConfounds). The plate segments are long but their gaps are player DELIBERATION over the pitch cards, not the catch->ready turnaround this constant models. The turnaround is a sub-second sliver after each pitch settles, readable only per-pitch frame-by-frame.
